content.text: A risk escalation path is the defined sequence of ownership levels, decision-making authorities, and response SLAs that a risk follows as its severity or score increases beyond what can be resolved at the current level. Without defined escalation paths, risks either stall in the team's risk log — where they receive no senior attention — or consume excessive steering committee time on minor issues that a programme manager could resolve.

Risk escalation paths define three things for every class of risk: the score threshold that triggers movement to the next governance level, the SLA within which the escalated risk must receive a decision, and the decision authority at each level. The standard model uses a 5x5 likelihood-impact matrix: likelihood scored 1 (rare) to 5 (almost certain), impact scored 1 (negligible) to 5 (catastrophic), yielding a score of 1 to 25.

The escalation thresholds in most enterprise AI programmes are: scores 1–8 handled by the delivery team, scores 9–15 escalated to programme management, scores 16–20 escalated to the steering committee, and scores above 20 (extremely high likelihood of catastrophic impact) escalated to the board or executive risk committee. Severity labels — Severity 1 (critical), Severity 2 (serious), Severity 3 (moderate) — overlay this matrix to determine SLA urgency independent of score.

Six risk categories apply to GenAI delivery: technical (model failures, integration defects), schedule (milestone delays, dependency slippage), budget (cost overruns, underfunded change control), vendor or third-party (API provider instability, SI delivery quality), regulatory or compliance (data protection, financial conduct rules, sector-specific AI regulations), and model quality or safety (hallucination rates, bias, concept drift, adversarial vulnerability).

AI programmes carry risk categories that standard software projects do not: model quality degradation, LLM provider outages, training data regulatory exposure, and emergent safety behaviours. These risks are difficult for delivery teams to assess against business impact — a model drift issue is a technical observation at the team level, but it is a regulatory exposure at the steering committee level. Without a defined path, the team either under-escalates (no one with authority acts) or over-escalates (every minor issue goes to the steering committee, which then disengages from risk reviews).

An energy company's AI programme prevented an estimated $8M regulatory penalty by escalating a model drift signal — flagged by an ML engineer — to the compliance committee via a defined Severity 1 path. The ML engineer's finding (model accuracy had degraded below the contractual threshold) triggered the same-day SLA, reached the compliance officer within 4 hours, and resulted in a voluntary disclosure to the regulator before the regulator identified the breach independently. Without the escalation path, the finding would have sat in the team's risk log for the next sprint review.
Severity 1 risks get same-day attention; Severity 3 risks do not clog the steering committee agenda.
Every risk has a single owner at each escalation level — no ambiguity about who is responsible for the decision.
Escalation events with timestamps, decisions, and assigned owners provide a defensible record for regulators and auditors.
A functioning escalation process makes it safe for engineers to surface risks without fear that they will be blamed for raising uncomfortable issues.
Aggregating escalated risks across projects gives steering committees a true risk picture of the AI programme portfolio.
Completing risk register entries with all required fields takes time that engineers often feel is a distraction from delivery.
Teams may set likelihood or impact scores conservatively to avoid triggering escalation for issues they prefer to handle internally.
Same-day Severity 1 SLAs require escalation recipients (steering committee members, compliance officers) to be reachable and responsive — this is a governance commitment that must be enforced.
Automated risk alerts that fire too frequently become noise. Threshold calibration requires tuning for each programme.

The delivery team identifies risks continuously — in daily standups, design reviews, retrospectives, and dependency tracking. Each risk is logged in the risk register with a title, category, likelihood score, impact score, computed risk score, current status, and owner. When the score exceeds the team-level threshold (score > 8), the owning programme manager is automatically notified and the risk status is updated to escalated.

Programme management reviews escalated risks in a weekly risk review meeting. Risks scored 9–15 are tracked, mitigation actions are assigned, and progress is reported to the steering committee in the programme health dashboard. Risks that score above 15 are escalated immediately to the steering committee chair with a risk brief — a 1-page summary of the risk, its score, the proposed mitigation, and the decision required.

Severity determines SLA, not just score. A risk scored 12 but classified as Severity 1 (e.g., it directly affects a live production system impacting customers) is escalated same-day regardless of score. Severity 2 risks have a 48-hour SLA for a decision. Severity 3 risks are reviewed at the next scheduled meeting (typically weekly).

The RACI for risk management is: delivery team — Responsible for identifying and logging; programme manager — Accountable for tracking and escalating; steering committee — Consulted on high scores; compliance and legal — Informed on regulatory category risks.

attrs.alt: Risk Escalation Funnel: team level (score 1-8, weekly), programme management (score 9-15, 48h SLA), steering committee (score 16-20, same-day Sev-1), board (score 20+). Risk types shown across bottom.

content.text: The escalation funnel narrows as severity increases — most risks are handled at the team level, a smaller number are escalated to programme management, and only the highest-impact risks reach the steering committee or board. The funnel structure prevents the common failure mode where all risks are treated as steering committee items, which paralyses both the steering committee and the delivery teams.
Any AI delivery programme in a regulated industry where model quality, data handling, or vendor dependencies create compliance exposure.
Programmes with more than 3 concurrent delivery teams, where risk visibility across teams requires a structured aggregation mechanism.
Programmes with external vendors or model API providers where SLA breaches, pricing changes, or capability regressions are material risks.
Any AI system that operates on decisions with material consequences for people — credit decisions, medical triage, claims processing — where model quality risks have direct regulatory implications.

Start with the risk register schema. Each entry must capture the fields needed to compute the score, determine the escalation level, and track SLA compliance. The YAML below shows a complete schema for a single risk entry, and a GitHub Actions workflow that alerts on unresolved high-severity risks.

# risk-register-entry.yaml  —  schema for a single risk entry
risk:
  id: RISK-0042
  title: "LLM model drift causing accuracy degradation in production claims classifier"
  category: model_quality_safety         # technical | schedule | budget | vendor | regulatory | model_quality_safety
  description: >
    Production model has not been re-evaluated against current claims data
    since initial deployment 90 days ago. Concept drift in incoming data
    may have degraded classification accuracy below the 95% threshold
    required by the SLA.
  identified_by: "Sarah Chen, ML Engineer"
  identified_date: "2026-07-10"
  likelihood: 4          # 1 (rare) – 5 (almost certain)
  impact: 4              # 1 (negligible) – 5 (catastrophic)
  score: 16              # likelihood x impact  →  escalation: steering_committee
  status: mitigating     # identified | mitigating | accepted | transferred | closed
  owner: "Programme Manager"
  escalation_level: steering_committee   # team | programme_management | steering_committee | board
  severity: 1            # 1 (critical, same-day SLA) | 2 (48h) | 3 (weekly)
  escalation_sla: same_day
  mitigation_actions:
    - action: "Run full evaluation suite on last 30-day production data"
      due: "2026-07-11"
      owner: "ML Engineer"
    - action: "Deploy shadow model with refreshed training data"
      due: "2026-07-14"
      owner: "ML Engineer"
    - action: "Present results to steering committee with go/no-go recommendation"
      due: "2026-07-15"
      owner: "Programme Manager"
  regulatory_implications: true
  regulatory_body: "FCA"
  notes: >
    If accuracy has degraded below 95%, the claims team may have been
    making incorrect routing decisions. Compliance must be notified
    immediately per the FCA model risk management policy.

# .github/workflows/risk-alert.yml
name: Risk Register Alert

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'governance/risk-register.yaml'
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 8 * * 1-5'   # weekday morning check

jobs:
  check-risk-register:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Parse risk register and alert on high severity
        id: risk-check
        run: |
          python3 - <<'PYEOF'
          import yaml, os, sys

          with open('governance/risk-register.yaml') as f:
              register = yaml.safe_load(f)

          alerts = []
          for risk in register.get('risks', []):
              score = risk.get('score', 0)
              status = risk.get('status', '')
              severity = risk.get('severity', 3)

              if score >= 16 and status not in ('accepted', 'closed', 'transferred'):
                  alerts.append({
                      'id': risk['id'],
                      'title': risk['title'],
                      'score': score,
                      'severity': severity,
                      'escalation_level': risk.get('escalation_level'),
                      'owner': risk.get('owner'),
                  })

          if alerts:
              print(f"ALERT: {len(alerts)} risks at score >= 16 require steering committee review")
              for a in alerts:
                  print(f"  [{a['id']}] Score={a['score']} Sev={a['severity']}: {a['title']}")
                  print(f"    Escalation: {a['escalation_level']} | Owner: {a['owner']}")
              sys.exit(1)   # fail the workflow → triggers Slack/Teams notification via branch rules
          else:
              print("Risk register check passed: no unresolved high-severity risks")
          PYEOF

      - name: Post to Slack if high-severity risks found
        if: failure()
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v2
        with:
          payload: |
            {
              "text": ":red_circle: *High-severity AI governance risks require immediate escalation*",
              "blocks": [{
                "type": "section",
                "text": {
                  "type": "mrkdwn",
                  "text": "One or more risks with score >= 16 are unresolved. Review risk register and escalate to steering committee.
*Repo:* ${{ github.repository }}
*Run:* ${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}"
                }
              }]
            }
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.RISK_ALERT_SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_TYPE: INCOMING_WEBHOOK

A fintech company's AI lending programme tracked a vendor risk when their primary credit bureau API provider announced a pricing model change that would increase per-call costs by 60% — enough to make the business case unprofitable. The risk was scored likelihood 4, impact 4, score 16, escalated to the steering committee within 48 hours. The committee authorised negotiation with two alternative providers, and a contract with a secondary provider was in place before the price change took effect.

A healthcare AI programme identified a schedule risk when a key data engineering dependency from a separate team was tracking 3 weeks late. Score: likelihood 5, impact 3, score 15 — programme management level. The programme manager negotiated a partial handoff (the dataset needed for the pilot only, not the full production set) that unblocked the delivery team without requiring steering committee involvement.

An energy company operating AI-assisted grid load forecasting identified model drift — the model's mean absolute percentage error had increased from 3.1% to 5.8% over 6 weeks — through automated monitoring. The ML engineer logged this as a model quality risk, scored it likelihood 4, impact 5 (the model directly informed bid pricing in a regulated energy market), score 20, and triggered the Severity 1 escalation path.

Within 4 hours, the compliance officer had been briefed. The compliance officer determined that the degraded forecast accuracy had caused the company to breach a regulatory accuracy requirement in 3 of the past 6 weekly submissions. Voluntary disclosure to the regulator was made that afternoon. The regulator reduced the penalty from an estimated $8M to under $200K on the basis that the company had identified, reported, and remediated the issue proactively. The model was retrained and redeployed within 5 business days under a formal change control process.
Applying the same weekly review cadence to a Severity 1 production model failure and a Severity 3 minor scope change destroys escalation credibility. Severity must determine urgency.
A risk register with 50 open risks and no status updates is worse than useless — it creates false assurance that risks are being tracked.
Letting teams self-assess scores without calibration:
Without calibration sessions, risk scores are inconsistent across teams. Run a scoring calibration exercise at the start of each programme.
Technical and schedule risks are well-understood; regulatory category risks require explicit prompting because engineers may not know when they have a compliance obligation to disclose.
When a risk is escalated, the team must be told what decision was made and why. Escalation paths with no feedback loop erode team trust in the process.
Run a risk identification workshop at the start of each phase, not just at project initiation. New risks emerge as scope becomes better understood.
Calibrate scores quarterly across programme teams using a set of reference risks with agreed scores — this keeps the 1–25 scale meaningful across the programme.
Automate the link between risk score threshold and notification — humans should not be deciding whether to escalate based on reading the register. The system should alert on threshold breach.
Require the risk owner to provide a 3-sentence mitigation summary when escalating — this prevents escalation being used as a way of dumping problems upward without proposed solutions.

Close risks explicitly. A risk with no clear closure criterion lingers indefinitely. Define closure criteria at the time of logging (e.g., 'closed when model accuracy returns to >= 95% for 14 consecutive days').

A risk register for a 10-person AI team might contain 20–40 active risks at any given time. At this scale, a YAML file or a Jira risk epic with sub-tasks is sufficient. For programmes with 5 or more teams, a shared register (Jira programme-level, ServiceNow, or a dedicated GRC tool like Archer) with automated scoring aggregation is necessary to give the programme manager visibility across teams.

GitHub Actions-based risk alerts (as shown above) are effective for development-phase risks where the register lives alongside code. For production operational risk monitoring (model drift, API availability, data quality), the risk trigger should come from observability tooling (Datadog, Grafana alerts) feeding into the risk register automatically via API, rather than requiring manual engineer logging.
Risk epics with custom fields for likelihood, impact, score, severity, and escalation level. Risk score field can drive Jira automation rules to create escalation tickets and notify owners.
Risk register template, escalation path documentation, and governance RACI stored as Confluence pages linked from each project's governance space.
Automated risk register checks on push and scheduled runs to detect unresolved high-severity risks and post Slack alerts.
For enterprises with existing GRC infrastructure, ServiceNow provides risk register, workflow, and escalation routing with full audit trail.
Monitoring dashboards that detect model quality degradation (accuracy drift, hallucination rate spikes) and create risk register entries automatically via Jira API.

Risk register entries frequently contain sensitive information: vendor contract terms, regulatory exposure assessments, model accuracy data that may be commercially sensitive, and internal capability gap assessments. The risk register must be access-controlled — restricted to programme governance participants, not visible to all development staff. In regulated industries, the register itself may be subject to discovery in regulatory investigations, so entries must be factual and free of speculative legal opinions that are not covered by privilege.

The escalation workflow must be resilient to personnel absence. If the risk owner or escalation target is on leave, a deputy must be defined. Same-day SLA for Severity 1 risks means the on-call governance contact must be reachable through multiple channels — Slack, phone, and email are all required as fallback options.

Think of a risk escalation path as a triage system in an emergency department. Patients (risks) are assessed on arrival (risk identification) and assigned a triage category (score and severity) that determines how quickly they are seen and by whom. A Severity 1 patient goes directly to the resuscitation bay — the most senior clinician available handles them immediately. A Severity 3 patient waits in the queue for the next available doctor. Not everything is an emergency; treating it as such breaks the system.

The critical discipline is honesty at triage. A triage nurse who under-classifies patients to keep the resuscitation bay quiet is creating risk, not reducing it. Similarly, a delivery team that under-scores risks to avoid steering committee attention is a governance failure mode that the programme manager must actively counter through calibration and sample audits of the register.
Risk score = likelihood (1–5) x impact (1–5). Thresholds: 1–8 team, 9–15 PM, 16–20 steering committee, 20+ board.
Severity (1–3) determines SLA urgency independent of score — a Severity 1 risk is same-day regardless of its score.
The RACI is non-negotiable: team identifies, PM tracks and escalates, steering committee decides, compliance and legal are informed on regulatory risks.
Automate the alert at the threshold — humans should not be deciding whether to escalate by reading the register; the system should notify on score breach.
Six risk categories require explicit tracking in AI programmes: technical, schedule, budget, vendor, regulatory/compliance, and model quality/safety.
Log-and-forget culture is the most common failure mode. Require status updates and define closure criteria at the time of risk logging.
An energy company prevented an $8M regulatory penalty by escalating model drift through a Severity 1 path to compliance within 4 hours of identification.
